summary: I was born in San Jose, California, on May 14, 1893. My birthplace was on Third Street, just off St. James Park. Years later, when I took my mother to a relative's funeral there, I asked her to show me the exact spot where I was born, which had become the site of the Osen Motor Sales Company garage.

The Osen Motor Sales Company garage was located on the site of my birthplace in San Jose. Interestingly, while I was in Reno, Nevada, I was actually putting a new floor in the Osen Motor Sales garage there. So, this company connected two important places in my life—San Jose and Reno.

I moved to Reno when I was in the eighth grade and attended the Southside School and later the old high school on West and Fifth Street. I graduated from high school there around Christmas of 1910 after three and a half years. Reno was also where I met my future wife, Bunny, who had lived in Pacific Grove when I did but was younger and we never met until Reno.

Shortly after I was born, my family moved to Watsonville, California, where we lived with my mother's parents, the Hamms. My grandfather, Jeremiah Hamm, trained horses for the San Francisco Fire Department and sold them to the department. I remember swimming in the Pajaro River and stealing watermelons and strawberries from the many Chinese residents there, as well as watching boats come in from San Francisco loaded with fruits and vegetables.

My grandfather Jeremiah Hamm made his living by training horses for the San Francisco Fire Department and then selling them to the department. We lived with him in Watsonville, California, during my early years. He was a significant figure in my childhood, connecting me to the fire department through his work.

My grandfather Jeremiah Hamm trained horses specifically for the San Francisco Fire Department and sold them to them. This connection gave me a unique perspective on the department through family ties. I also recall the San Francisco earthquake of 1906 and the fires that followed, which I could see from Pacific Grove.

As a child in Watsonville, California, I spent a lot of time swimming in the Pajaro River. It was part of my early adventures along with stealing fruits from local Chinese residents. The river was a natural playground during those days.

We moved to Cripple Creek, Colorado, when I was young, and all I really remember from there was digging little holes and pretending to have my own mine with a headframe. It was a brief and simple memory compared to other places we lived.

After Cripple Creek, we moved to Colorado Springs where my grandfather Edward Ferris lived. He ran a linen and silk business with branches in Colorado Springs and Philadelphia and traveled to Europe annually to buy linens. I have fond memories of playing pinochle with him and taking long walks to places like the Garden of the Gods. I also recall the Antlers Hotel burning down during our time there and the celebration of the century bells at midnight on January 1, 1900.

My grandfather Edward Ferris had a linen and silk establishment with stores in Colorado Springs and Philadelphia. He traveled to Europe every year to purchase linens. I remember playing pinochle with him and taking walks together, including to the Garden of the Gods. He was a prominent figure in my life during our time in Colorado Springs.

Philadelphia was one of the cities where my grandfather Edward Ferris sold his linens and silk. Though I did not live there, it was part of his business operations alongside Colorado Springs. His trips to Europe for buying linens were connected to his work in Philadelphia.

My grandfather Edward Ferris made annual trips to Europe to buy linens for his business in Colorado Springs and Philadelphia. These trips were an important part of his work and helped sustain the family business. I remember hearing about these journeys as part of his trade.

While living in Colorado Springs, I took long walks with my grandfather Edward Ferris to the Garden of the Gods. I vividly remember being taken by my father to the top of the highest rock there, looking down a narrow ledge that seemed to drop nine miles to the ground. It was an awe-inspiring place that left a lasting impression on me.

During our time in Colorado Springs, the main hotel, the Antlers Hotel, burned down in a spectacular fire. It was a large wooden building, and the fire spread to a nearby lumber yard, making it quite a spectacle to witness. This event stuck in my memory as a dramatic moment of that period.

We moved to Trinidad, Colorado, a coal mining town, where I witnessed some unique local customs like the Penitentes' annual festival involving self-whipping with thorny twigs. I also remember sledding down a steep street to the railroad station and the mischief kids got into by freezing water on sidewalks to skate, which caused many injuries and was eventually stopped. Trinidad was a place of vivid childhood memories and adventures.

In Trinidad, Colorado, I saw the Penitentes, a group of Indians who held a private yearly festival where they whipped themselves on their bare backs with thorny twigs. We kids hid in the sagebrush to watch, careful not to get too close. It was a strange and memorable cultural event from my childhood.

We moved to Pacific Grove, California, where my father worked as a sign painter and played flute in the local orchestra. I learned to fish there and earned money cleaning fishermen's boats. I also played games like hare and hounds, often ending up in Carmel-by-the-Sea, and I remember the deserted Carmel Mission and shipwrecks along the coast. I was in Pacific Grove until about 1906, when we moved after the San Francisco earthquake.

As a child in Pacific Grove, I often played hare and hounds, and we usually ended up in Carmel-by-the-Sea. Around 1905, Carmel Mission was a deserted building with one priest and the smell of dead animals. I also remember exploring shipwrecks nearby, including one loaded with telephone poles that I tried to cross while being chased during a game.

We moved from Pacific Grove to Reno right after the San Francisco earthquake of 1906. From a tower on a stable in Pacific Grove, I could see the smoke from the fires caused by the earthquake in San Francisco. It was a significant event that marked a turning point in our lives.

San Francisco was connected to my life through the fire department that my grandfather Jeremiah Hamm supplied horses to, and also through the 1906 earthquake and fires that I witnessed the smoke of from Pacific Grove. Additionally, boats came from San Francisco to Watsonville to load fruits and vegetables when I was a child. The city was a backdrop to many events in my early years.

When we moved to Reno, I attended the Southside School during my eighth grade year before moving on to the old high school on West and Fifth Street. This was the start of my formal education in Reno, where I spent my adolescent years and graduated high school in 1910.

Dr. Ed Dopey Chase was the principal of the high school I attended in Reno. After a physics class prank involving electrical wires went wrong, I ended up having to study in his office and run messages for teachers. He was a memorable figure in my school life, especially because I got blamed for the incident that happened right over his office.

E. E. Winfrey was the principal at my high school in Reno. I once got into trouble with him after throwing a water cooler during a wrestling match in the school hall. He called me a whelp and an imp of Satan, which I took seriously enough to hit him and then get barred from school, leading to a required apology I initially refused to give.

Miss Goodwin Doten was a teacher at my high school in Reno who had study periods. After I was barred from school for hitting Principal Winfrey, I was required to apologize to her, but I initially refused. She was related to the Doten family associated with the University, which made her a notable figure in my school experience.

Baltimore was mentioned in relation to the Quakers and their history, but I did not have a personal connection or specific memories tied to Baltimore in my early life. It was part of the broader context of places connected to my family background.
Wilmington, Delaware, was another place connected to my family's Quaker background, but I did not have direct experiences there. It was part of the historical and cultural context of my ancestors.

My family had connections to the Quakers, which linked us to places like Baltimore and Wilmington, Delaware. While I did not grow up practicing the faith, the Quaker heritage was part of my family history and cultural identity.

Swarthmore College was mentioned as part of the Quaker educational tradition connected to my family, but I did not attend or have a direct relationship with the college. It was part of the broader cultural background of my ancestors.

Kit Carson was referenced in relation to Colorado history, but I did not have personal experiences with him. His name came up in stories connected to the places I lived, like Colorado Springs and Trinidad.

The Black Panther Mine was not directly mentioned in my personal stories, but it was part of the mining context in places like Cripple Creek and Trinidad, Colorado, where I spent parts of my childhood. My own mining memories were more about playing with little holes and headframes.
